input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Physical system is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, designed for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining operations. Key components include motors, gearboxes, and precision actuators, operating in an industrial manufacturing environment requiring high precision and dynamic motion control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Services include real-time monitoring and visualization via dashboard interfaces, friction identification for dynamic adjustment of robot dynamics, and optimization for enhancing motion accuracy through sensor data analysis and simulation-based corrective actions. Target users are manufacturing engineers and maintenance personnel.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML for system structure and behavior modeling, RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for handling complexity across abstraction levels. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are employed for component & connector perspectives, defining system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is enhanced using MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and code in general-purpose languages such as C++ or Java for detailed behavior description. These tools collectively address heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and integrate multiple modeling notations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include structural components via SysML and Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs, defining components, interfaces, and interconnections across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical abstraction layers. Behavioral modeling utilizes state machines in SysML v1.7, MontiArc Automaton, MathWorks Simulink simulations, and general-purpose code in C++ or Java. Data originates from physical systems through automated data flows, with heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives integrated using methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, a gear transmission, and a cycloidal gear. Control is managed by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive, with the motor encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. Sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Live measurements from the robot's sensors are streamed via MQTT protocol as time series data to the digital system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication infrastructure employs MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized data exchange and interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for creating Digital Shadows follows a structured four-step methodology: (1) Problem identification to define specific use case requirements, (2) Asset and model analysis to evaluate existing physical assets and potential digital representations, (3) Use or creation of calculations or models to develop computational components for data processing, and (4) Data identification to specify required data sources and integration points for the digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Insights include friction loss detection as defined in Section IV.A, with computational processing performed by a dedicated Processing component in the Digital Shadow reference model (Section II.B) that interprets and analyzes incoming data streams. User interaction is facilitated through dashboard visualizations described in the Abstract, providing real-time data representation for informed decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system utilizes industry-standard modeling languages such as SysML, AADL, ACME, and MontiArc for architecture description and component-based modeling. Methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied to manage abstraction levels across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. Behavior specification is achieved through tools like MathWorks Simulink and programming languages such as C++ or Java.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description correctly identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and its use for 'complex manufacturing tasks such as machining', which is directly supported by the source: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100... it is suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'. It mentions components like motors and gearboxes; the text confirms 'the first axis... is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor... a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear'. The operational domain 'industrial manufacturing environment requiring high precision' is supported by the context of high-precision machining. The extraction is highly specific and well-grounded.

The extraction lists 'real-time monitoring and visualization via dashboard interfaces', which is supported by the text: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard'. It mentions 'friction identification for dynamic adjustment of robot dynamics', aligning with the goal to 'derive the friction... within the gearbox... to improve the robot control’s accuracy'. The 'optimization for enhancing motion accuracy through sensor data analysis and simulation-based corrective actions' is supported by the comparison of 'reference values and simulated values' and the use of 'executable simulation models'. However, 'target users are manufacturing engineers and maintenance personnel' is not explicitly found in the text, and 'dynamic adjustment' implies a control action not fully detailed in the snippets (which focus on identification and understanding). The extraction is good but includes unsupported details about target users.

The extraction provides a comprehensive and detailed summary of the tools and enablers discussed in Section II.A of the source text. It accurately lists MBSE methodologies (SysML, RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), Architecture Description Languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), and behavior modeling tools (MontiArc Automaton, MathWorks Simulink, C++, Java). The extraction is highly specific and well-grounded in the provided evidence.
